extra.message:
Data leakage risk: marks retrieval not scoped to authenticated user/permissions

`get_for_student()` returns all marks for an arbitrary `student_id` with no authorization/ownership checks. If a handler allows users to supply `student_id`, they can access other students' grades (highly sensitive PII/education records).

Impact: Unauthorized disclosure of students' grades/academic records; may violate privacy regulations and enable targeted harassment or discrimination.

Fix: Require caller to pass an authorization scope (e.g., current_user_id/role, school_id) and enforce it in the query (join to enrollment/permissions). At minimum, ensure handlers verify the requester is the student or an authorized admin/teacher before calling.

CSRF protection middleware is commented out (CSRF disabled globally)

The CSRF middleware is present but not enabled in the application's middleware chain. All POST endpoints (login, create student/course, evaluate, review, logout) are therefore vulnerable to cross-site request forgery.

Impact: An attacker can force a victim’s browser to submit authenticated POST requests (e.g., create/modify data, logout) without the victim’s consent.

Fix: Enable csrf_middleware (or a robust CSRF library) for all state-changing requests. Use per-session tokens, validate Origin/Referer as defense-in-depth, and ensure tokens are not single-use unless carefully designed.

Jinja2 autoescape disabled enables stored/reflected XSS across all templates

Jinja2 is configured with autoescape=False, so any template variable rendered into HTML will not be escaped by default. Because multiple endpoints accept user-controlled input (e.g., student name, course title/description, review_text, login errors) that is later rendered in templates, this creates application-wide XSS risk.

Impact: Attackers can inject arbitrary HTML/JS that executes in victims' browsers, enabling session theft, CSRF, account takeover, and data exfiltration (PII).

Fix: Enable autoescaping (autoescape=True) and ensure templates use proper escaping. For any intentional HTML rendering, use explicit safe-marking on trusted content only. Add output encoding tests and consider a CSP.

SQL injection via string interpolation in INSERT query (students.create)

The INSERT statement is built using Python string formatting with untrusted input (name) embedded directly inside quotes. An attacker can inject SQL by including quote characters and SQL payloads in the name value.

Impact: Arbitrary SQL execution (read/modify/delete data, bypass auth checks implemented in SQL, potentially achieve RCE via DB extensions depending on DB configuration).

Fix: Use parameterized queries: `await cur.execute('INSERT INTO students (name) VALUES (%s)', (name,))` (or named parameters supported by the driver) and never format SQL with `%`/f-strings for user input.

XSS risk: course title/description/review_text rendered without escaping in course detail view

Multiple user-controlled fields are rendered without explicit escaping: course.title, course.description, and review.review_text. Reviews are typically user-generated content, making stored XSS highly likely if autoescape is disabled.

Impact: Stored XSS via course reviews can compromise any user viewing the course page (including admins), enabling session theft and privilege escalation.

Fix: Enable autoescape globally. Escape all user-generated fields (|e). If rich text is required, sanitize on input/output and only mark sanitized HTML as safe.

Missing authentication/authorization on student creation endpoint (unauthenticated POST)

The students handler allows POST requests to create new students without any authentication or authorization checks.

Impact: Any unauthenticated user can create/poison student records, potentially leading to stored XSS (via name) and data integrity compromise.

Fix: Require authentication (and likely admin/teacher role) for creating students. Enforce authorization in the handler (e.g., @authorize(ensure_admin=True)). Add server-side validation and CSRF protection.

Potential SQL injection via DAO calls using user-controlled fields (create student/course/review/mark)

User-controlled POST fields are passed directly into DAO create methods. If those DAO methods build SQL via string formatting (common in intentionally vulnerable apps named 'sqli'), this becomes SQL injection. The sink is the DAO execute/query, but from this chunk the unsafe flow is evident and should be treated as high risk.

Impact: SQL injection could allow data exfiltration/modification, authentication bypass, and potentially RCE via database features depending on configuration.

Fix: Ensure all DAO queries use parameterized statements (e.g., aiopg/psycopg2 placeholders) and never string-concatenate SQL. Add input validation and least-privilege DB accounts.

POST /students/ allows unauthenticated creation of students (missing authentication)

The students handler accepts POST requests and creates Student records without checking for an authenticated user (no @authorize, no session/user check).

Impact: Anonymous attackers can create arbitrary student records (data integrity compromise) and potentially inject stored XSS payloads into names (given autoescape=False).

Fix: Require authentication (and likely admin role) for creating students; enforce server-side authorization checks in the POST branch.

Stored XSS risk: unvalidated student name accepted and later rendered with autoescape disabled

Student name is accepted from POST without validation/sanitization. With global autoescape disabled, any HTML/JS stored in the name will execute when rendered in templates listing students or showing a student.

Impact: Persistent XSS leading to session theft and account takeover.

Fix: Enable autoescape; validate/sanitize name (length, allowed characters) and/or HTML-escape on output.

Weak password hashing using unsalted MD5 for password verification

Passwords are verified by comparing the stored hash to an unsalted MD5 digest of the provided password. MD5 is fast and broken for password hashing, enabling efficient offline brute-force/cracking if the password hash database is leaked.

Impact: If an attacker obtains the users table (via SQLi, backup leak, etc.), they can crack many user passwords quickly and reuse them for account takeover; also enables credential stuffing across services.

Fix: Use a modern password hashing scheme with per-user salt and work factor (Argon2id, bcrypt, scrypt, or PBKDF2). Store algorithm parameters with the hash and use constant-time verification helpers from a vetted library (e.g., passlib).

Session cookie configured with HttpOnly disabled (client-side script can read session)

RedisStorage is created with httponly=False, causing the session cookie to be accessible to JavaScript. Combined with XSS, this makes session theft trivial.

Impact: If any XSS occurs (likely due to autoescape=False), attackers can read and exfiltrate session cookies, leading to account takeover.

Fix: Set httponly=True for session cookies. Also set Secure and SameSite attributes (e.g., Secure=True, SameSite=Lax/Strict) and consider rotating session IDs on login.

Error pages render exception object into templates with autoescape disabled (XSS/info leak amplification)

The error handlers pass the exception object into templates as 'error'. With autoescape disabled globally, any exception message containing user-controlled content (e.g., from malformed input, path, query) could be rendered unescaped, enabling reflected XSS. Additionally, exception details may leak internal information.

Impact: Reflected XSS via error messages and potential disclosure of internal exception details/stack context depending on templates.

Fix: Enable autoescape; avoid rendering raw exception objects/messages to users. Render generic error messages and log details server-side.

Redis connection created without authentication/TLS options in schema and service

Redis configuration schema lacks fields for password/ACL username and TLS settings. This encourages unauthenticated, plaintext Redis connections and makes it impossible to configure authentication via the validated config.

Impact: If Redis is reachable by other containers/hosts, attackers can read/modify session data or application state stored in Redis, potentially leading to account takeover.

Fix: Extend schema to include Redis auth (username/password) and TLS parameters; enforce presence in non-dev environments. Configure Redis with ACLs and requirepass, and restrict network exposure.

No CSRF protection configuration evident in this chunk (likely missing/disabled CSRF)

The configuration schema contains no CSRF-related settings (secret, enabled flag, trusted origins) and this project context indicates CSRF middleware may be missing/disabled. While the actual middleware is elsewhere, the config layer provides no way to enable/enforce CSRF protections per environment.

Impact: State-changing requests may be forgeable from attacker-controlled sites, leading to unauthorized actions under a victim's session.

Fix: Add CSRF configuration (enabled by default, secret key, trusted origins) and ensure CSRF middleware is enabled for all unsafe HTTP methods. Provide environment-specific overrides.

Redis pool created without authentication/TLS (unauthenticated Redis access possible)

The Redis client pool is created using only host/port/db with no password/ACL or TLS configuration. If Redis is exposed on the network, anyone can connect and manipulate data (e.g., sessions).

Impact: Session tampering, data exfiltration, cache poisoning, and potential RCE in some Redis misuse scenarios; account takeover if sessions stored in Redis.

Fix: Configure Redis authentication (ACL user/pass) and TLS; pass credentials to the client. Ensure Redis is bound to private network only and protected by firewall/security groups.

Reflected/stored XSS risk: unescaped auth_user name fields rendered in navbar (autoescape may be disabled)

The template renders user-controlled profile fields (first/middle/last name) directly into HTML without explicit escaping. If Jinja2 autoescaping is disabled (noted in app context), an attacker can store HTML/JS in their name fields and trigger XSS for any viewer (including admins).

Impact: Account takeover via session theft, CSRF via injected requests, credential phishing, and arbitrary actions as the victim user.

Fix: Enable Jinja2 autoescape globally for HTML templates. Additionally, explicitly escape user-controlled fields: {{ auth_user.first_name|e }} etc. Consider output encoding for attributes and enforce server-side validation/allowlist for name fields.

Missing CSRF token in admin evaluate-student POST forms

The evaluate form performs a state-changing POST to /students/{id}/evaluate/{course.id} but does not include a CSRF token hidden field. This enables CSRF against an authenticated admin (or any user with access) if server-side CSRF protection is not enforced.

Impact: An attacker can trick an admin into submitting evaluations/grades without consent, altering student marks.

Fix: Include CSRF token input in the form (e.g., <input type="hidden" name="_csrf_token" value="{{ csrf_token() }}">) and enforce CSRF validation server-side for all state-changing requests.

XSS risk: courses list renders course.title and course.description without escaping

Course title and description are rendered into HTML without explicit escaping. With autoescape disabled, attacker-controlled course fields can inject scripts (stored XSS).

Impact: Stored XSS affecting all users browsing courses; can lead to session theft and account takeover.

Fix: Enable autoescape; escape fields (|e). If descriptions allow formatting, sanitize with an HTML sanitizer and render safe only after sanitization.

Sensitive data exposure: internal error object __dict__ rendered to users on 50x page

The template prints `{{ error.__dict__ }}` which can include stack/context details, configuration, headers, cookies, or other sensitive internals. This is an information disclosure sink in rendered HTML.

Impact: Leaks internal implementation details and potentially secrets/PII, aiding further exploitation (SQLi, auth bypass, session attacks).

Fix: Remove detailed error rendering in production. Show a generic message and log details server-side. If debugging is needed, gate behind admin-only access and sanitize output.

XSS risk: course.title rendered without escaping in review form heading

course.title is rendered without explicit escaping in the form heading. If autoescape is disabled and course.title is attacker-controlled, this is a stored XSS sink.

Impact: Stored XSS on the review page; can compromise users writing reviews.

Fix: Escape course.title ({{ course.title|e }}) and enable autoescape.

XSS risk: course title/description rendered without escaping in student results view

course.title and course.description are rendered without explicit escaping. If these fields are attacker-controlled (e.g., created by admin or compromised account) and autoescape is disabled, this becomes stored XSS.

Impact: Stored XSS on student detail pages; can compromise users viewing results.

Fix: Enable autoescape and explicitly escape: {{ course.title|e }}, {{ course.description|e }}. Consider sanitizing rich text if HTML is intended.

XSS risk: students list renders id and name without escaping (href attribute + text)

Student id is interpolated into an href attribute and student name into element text without explicit escaping. If autoescape is disabled and either value is attacker-controlled (e.g., stored in DB), this can lead to XSS (including attribute-breaking payloads like '" onmouseover="...').

Impact: Stored XSS affecting any user viewing the students list; can target admins and lead to privilege escalation.

Fix: Enable autoescape; additionally apply |e for text and |urlencode for path segments (or build URLs server-side). Validate that id is numeric/UUID and name is sanitized/validated.

Template context processor injects full auth_user model into all templates (sensitive data exposure risk)

auth_user_processor injects the full User object into every template context. If the User model contains sensitive fields (password hash, email, roles, tokens, internal IDs), they cross a trust boundary and may be accidentally rendered or exposed via template debugging.

Impact: Accidental disclosure of sensitive user attributes in rendered HTML; increased blast radius if any template is compromised.

Fix: Inject only a safe subset (e.g., id, username, display_name, is_admin). Avoid passing full ORM/DAO objects to templates.

No rate limiting / brute-force protection on login endpoint

The login handler accepts unlimited POST attempts with username/password. This enables online brute force and credential stuffing.

Impact: Account compromise via password guessing; increased load/DoS risk.

Fix: Add rate limiting per IP/username, exponential backoff, and account lockout/alerts as appropriate.

No session ID regeneration on login (session fixation)

After successful authentication, the code sets session['user_id'] but does not regenerate/rotate the session identifier. An attacker who can set or predict a victim’s session ID (or force a victim to use an attacker-known session) can fixate the session and then hijack it after login.

Impact: Session fixation can allow account takeover after the victim logs in using an attacker-controlled session.

Fix: Regenerate/rotate the session ID upon successful login (e.g., invalidate old session and create a new one). Clear any pre-auth session data as appropriate.

Application runs with debug=True (potential information disclosure / unsafe debug behavior)

The aiohttp Application is created with debug=True. In public-facing deployments this can increase information leakage via verbose errors and may enable debug behaviors that aid attackers.

Impact: May expose stack traces, internal configuration, and code paths that facilitate exploitation of other vulnerabilities.

Fix: Disable debug in production; control via environment/config and ensure error handling returns generic messages.

Data leakage risk: unscoped bulk listing of students without authorization/ownership constraints

`get_many()` returns all students (optionally paginated) with no filtering by the requesting user/tenant/permissions. In a web app, using this DAO from a handler without strict authorization can leak PII (student names/IDs) across users.

Impact: Any endpoint using this DAO may expose the full student directory to unauthorized users, enabling privacy violations and user enumeration.

Fix: Add scoping parameters (e.g., organization_id/owner_id) and enforce them in the WHERE clause. Ensure handlers pass the authenticated user context and apply authorization checks before calling bulk-list DAOs.

Overexposure of sensitive user fields (pwd_hash, is_admin) in User DAO retrieval methods

User.get() and User.get_by_username() select and return `pwd_hash` and `is_admin` as part of the User object. If this object is ever passed to templates/API responses without strict filtering, it can leak password hashes and privilege flags. This is a common data exposure footgun in DAO/model layers.

Impact: Password hash disclosure enables offline cracking; leaking `is_admin` can aid targeted attacks and privilege escalation attempts; increases blast radius of any serialization bug.

Fix: Apply least-privilege data access: create separate DTOs/views for public profile vs authentication/authorization. Avoid returning `pwd_hash` except in authentication-specific code paths; never serialize it. Consider splitting User into `UserPublic` and `UserAuth` models or adding explicit serialization whitelists.

Sensitive credential material (password hash) included in User model returned from DAO

The DAO selects and returns `pwd_hash` as part of the `User` object in both `get()` and `get_by_username()`. Returning password hashes broadly increases the risk of accidental exposure (e.g., passing full User objects into templates, logs, or JSON responses).

Impact: Password hashes may be inadvertently disclosed to clients via template context, debug pages, serialization, or logging, enabling offline cracking and account compromise.

Fix: Do not include `pwd_hash` in general-purpose user DTOs. Create a separate internal auth-only query/object for password verification, or return a User object without sensitive fields. Ensure any rendering/serialization uses an allowlist of safe fields.

Missing Secure/SameSite attributes on session cookies (cookie hardening absent)

Session cookie configuration only sets httponly (and sets it to False). There is no explicit Secure or SameSite configuration shown. In a public deployment, cookies should be marked Secure (HTTPS only) and SameSite (Lax/Strict) to reduce theft and CSRF risk.

Impact: Cookies may be sent over non-HTTPS if the site is accessible via HTTP and are more likely to be included in cross-site requests, increasing CSRF/session theft risk.

Fix: Configure session cookies with Secure=True (require HTTPS) and SameSite=Lax/Strict. Ensure the app is served only over HTTPS and consider HSTS at the reverse proxy.

Database connection DSN built with plaintext password and no TLS enforcement

The PostgreSQL DSN includes a password and does not specify SSL/TLS parameters (e.g., sslmode=require). This can result in credentials and data being transmitted in cleartext if the DB connection traverses an untrusted network or is misconfigured.

Impact: Potential interception of DB credentials and sensitive data in transit; increased risk if containers are deployed across hosts/VPCs or if traffic is routed externally.

Fix: Enforce TLS by adding sslmode=require/verify-full and providing CA certs as needed. Prefer libpq keyword args or DSN with explicit SSL settings; ensure DB is configured to require SSL.

Database DSN built with plaintext password string (risk of credential exposure via logs/errors)

The DSN string is constructed to include the database password in plaintext. While not directly logged here, DSNs are commonly logged in exceptions/debug output, increasing the chance of credential disclosure.

Impact: Credential leakage through stack traces, debug logs, APM instrumentation, or error reporting systems.

Fix: Avoid embedding passwords in DSN strings where possible; pass credentials as separate parameters if supported, or ensure DSN is never logged/redacted in error handlers. Use secret storage and minimize debug logging in production.

XSS risk: evaluation error page renders errors dict without escaping

The template renders `errors` directly. If errors include user input (e.g., invalid points) and autoescape is disabled, this can lead to reflected XSS.

Impact: Reflected XSS on evaluation error page; could target admins during evaluation workflows.

Fix: Escape both fieldname and error messages (|e) and avoid embedding raw user input in error strings. Enable autoescape.

XSS risk: last_visited rendered without escaping (autoescape may be disabled)

The template renders last_visited directly. If this value can be influenced (e.g., via cookie, header, query param, or stored value) and autoescape is disabled, it becomes an XSS sink.

Impact: Potential reflected XSS on the home page depending on how last_visited is sourced.

Fix: Ensure last_visited is a server-generated datetime and not user-controlled; still apply escaping ({{ last_visited|e }}) and enable global autoescape.

Logout endpoint lacks CSRF protection (forced logout) due to disabled CSRF middleware

Although logout requires authentication via @authorize, it is a POST endpoint and CSRF middleware is disabled globally, so a third-party site can force a victim to POST /logout/ and terminate their session.

Impact: Forced logout (session disruption) and potential user annoyance; can be used in broader attack chains.

Fix: Re-enable CSRF middleware and/or require a CSRF token for logout; consider SameSite cookies.

Missing input validation and size limits on course fields enables injection and resource abuse

courses() accepts title/description without validation or size limits.

Impact: DoS via large payloads; increased injection/XSS risk.

Fix: Add schema validation (trafaret) for title/description with max lengths; enforce request size limits.

POST /courses/ lacks rate limiting (unbounded record creation / abuse)

No throttling on course creation allows automated spam/DoS via mass course creation.

Impact: Database bloat and service degradation.

Fix: Add rate limiting/quotas and validate input lengths.

Potential SQL injection / query manipulation risk from dynamic LIMIT/OFFSET construction and incorrect parameter passing (courses.get_many)

The query is dynamically concatenated with LIMIT/OFFSET fragments and then executed with `await cur.execute(q, **params)`. Passing parameters via `**params` is atypical for DB-API execute and may cause parameters to be ignored or mishandled, increasing the chance of developers switching to unsafe string formatting to 'fix' it. Dynamic SQL concatenation also increases injection risk if future changes incorporate user-controlled fragments.

Impact: If parameters are not bound as intended, the query may error or behave unexpectedly; future patches may introduce direct interpolation leading to SQL injection. If any part becomes user-controlled SQL, could lead to arbitrary SQL execution.

Fix: Use correct parameter binding: `await cur.execute(q, params)` (dict) or positional placeholders. Avoid concatenating SQL; build a fixed query with optional clauses using safe patterns and strict int casting for limit/offset.

CSRF middleware uses single-use token (pop) and is not enabled; design may cause usability issues and token desync

The CSRF middleware (currently not enabled) pops the token from the session on every POST, making tokens single-use and prone to desynchronization with multiple tabs/retries. While not directly exploitable, it can lead to users disabling protections or inconsistent behavior.

Impact: Operational/usability issues that can reduce effective CSRF protection if enabled without adjustment.

Fix: Use a stable per-session token (do not pop) or implement double-submit/rotating tokens with proper synchronization; keep middleware enabled.

Input validation schemas allow unbounded review_text/description size (potential abuse of resources)

`review_text` has only a minimum length and no maximum; `description` has no max length. Attackers can submit very large payloads leading to memory/DB bloat and potential DoS.

Impact: Resource exhaustion (CPU/memory/storage), degraded performance, increased costs.

Fix: Add reasonable max_length limits (and server request body size limits) for all text fields; validate and truncate as appropriate.

CSRF token presence depends on csrf_token() global; risk of false sense of protection if not enforced server-side

Templates include a hidden _csrf_token using csrf_token() in some forms. If server-side CSRF middleware/validation is disabled, these tokens provide no protection and may mislead developers. (Also note at least one POST form lacks the token entirely.)

Impact: If CSRF validation is not enforced, state-changing actions remain vulnerable to CSRF despite token fields.

Fix: Ensure CSRF is enforced server-side for all POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE requests; add tokens to every form and verify them.

XSS risk: error.status rendered without escaping in error page title and image URL

error.status is rendered without explicit escaping in the title and in an image URL path segment. It is likely numeric and server-controlled, but if attacker-influenced and autoescape is disabled, it could be abused for HTML/attribute injection.

Impact: Low likelihood XSS; more likely a hardening issue.

Fix: Ensure status is an int; still escape (|e) and/or cast to int in handler before rendering.

XSS risk: error.status rendered without escaping in error page title (50x) and image URL

`error.status` is rendered without escaping in title and image URL. Usually numeric, but with autoescape disabled this is a potential injection sink if status is not strictly controlled.

Impact: Low likelihood injection; hardening.

Fix: Coerce status to int and escape; enable autoescape.

XSS risk: error rendering bug may leak/reflect unexpected content in review form

Template iterates `{% for fieldname, errors in errors.items %}` but prints `{{ error }}` (undefined). Depending on Jinja configuration, undefined variables may render as empty or raise exceptions; if exceptions are shown, this can disclose template internals. Also, if later fixed to print error content, it should be escaped to avoid XSS.

Impact: Potential information disclosure via template errors/stack traces in misconfigured environments; future XSS risk if error content is rendered unescaped.

Fix: Fix variable name (iterate errors list and print each), ensure escaping (|e), and ensure production hides template exceptions.

Authorization helper fetches user by session user_id without validating type/None (potential auth confusion)

get_auth_user() reads user_id from session and passes it to User.get(). If templates render auth_user fields with autoescape disabled, any attacker-controlled user record fields could lead to XSS. Additionally, lack of validation on user_id type/None can cause unexpected behavior depending on DAO implementation.

Impact: Potential for unexpected auth state and, combined with unsafe template rendering, XSS via user profile fields.

Fix: Validate session user_id (ensure int), handle None explicitly, and ensure templates escape user fields; enable autoescape.

CSRF token generation exists but is ineffective because CSRF middleware is disabled

A CSRF token is generated and stored in session, but without the csrf_middleware enabled, tokens are not enforced. This can create a false sense of security and may lead to incomplete protection.

Impact: Developers may assume CSRF is protected when it is not.

Fix: Enable csrf_middleware and ensure all forms include the token; add tests to verify CSRF enforcement.
